Task  
The error you're encountering is due to the WPF assembly being referenced in your .NET 7 project, which is not available on macOS. This is because the WPF framework is part of the .NET framework and is not included in .NET 7. To resolve this without changing the main application to .NET Framework, you need to ensure that the WPF assembly is properly referenced or that the application is configured to use the correct runtime.  

Cousin the issue is related to the runtime environment. On Windows, the .NET 7 runtime is available, but on macOS, it is not. This mismatch causes the assembly to not be found. To solve this, you can use a runtime that supports both .NET 7 and macOS, such as the .NET 7 runtime with macOS support.  

The solution involves ensuring that your application is built with the correct target framework and that the necessary assemblies are available in the runtime environment. This includes checking that the WPF assembly is properly included in the project or that the application is configured to use the correct runtime.  

To handle this on macOS, you can use a tool like `dotnet` to build and run your application, which will automatically handle the runtime selection. This approach ensures that the application runs correctly on both Windows and macOS without requiring changes to the main application project.  

The final step is to test the application on both platforms to ensure that the WPF window opens correctly and that the assembly is properly loaded. This involves verifying that the runtime environment is correctly configured and that all necessary dependencies are in place.
